title: Enterprise-Wide Cyber Risk Management
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure that there is an enterprise-wide focus on effective cyber risk management to reflect the collective responsibility of business and technology lines for managing cyber risks.
translation: A financial institution must manage cyber risk as an enterprise-wide responsibility, ensuring that both business and technology lines share collective accountability for effective cyber risk management across the organisation.
evidence: Enterprise Cyber Risk Management Framework
Cyber Risk Governance Committee Terms of Reference and Meeting Records
Business Line Cyber Risk Register with Ownership Attribution
Enterprise Cyber Risk Reporting to Board and Senior Management
Cyber Risk Training and Awareness Records for Business Lines

artifacts.name: Incident Response Plan
artifacts.description: Comprehensive IRP covering identification, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activities

artifacts.name: IRP Testing Records
artifacts.description: Records of IRP testing through tabletop exercises or simulations

artifacts.name: Post-Incident Review Reports
artifacts.description: Reports from post-incident reviews with lessons learned and improvements

title: Cyber Resilience Framework Development
verbatim: A financial institution must develop a CRF which clearly articulates its governance for managing cyber risks, its cyber resilience objectives and its risk tolerance, with due regard to the evolving cyber threat environment. Objectives of the CRF shall include ensuring operational resilience against extreme but plausible cyber-attacks. The framework must be able to support the effective identification, protection, detection, response, and recovery (IPDRR) of systems and data hosted on-premises or by third party service providers from internal and external cyber-attacks.
translation: A financial institution must develop a Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) that clearly states its governance approach, cyber resilience objectives, and risk tolerance in light of the evolving threat environment, including the goal of operational resilience against extreme but plausible cyber-attacks. The CRF must support the full identify, protect, detect, respond, and recover (IPDRR) lifecycle for systems and data held on-premises or by third-party service providers.
evidence: Board-Approved Cyber Resilience Framework (CRF) Policy Document
CRF Governance Accountability and Oversight Records
IPDRR Control Mapping and Implementation Evidence
Cyber Risk Tolerance Statement and Risk Assessment Records
Threat Intelligence and Threat Environment Assessment Records

artifacts.name: Cybersecurity Strategy
artifacts.description: Board-approved cybersecurity strategy with vision, objectives, and roadmap

artifacts.name: Board Strategy Approval
artifacts.description: Board approval records for the cybersecurity strategy

artifacts.name: Strategy Review Report
artifacts.description: Annual review report on cybersecurity strategy effectiveness and updates

title: CRF Minimum Elements
translation: The CRF must contain at least nine elements: an institutional understanding of cyber risk context and posture; identification and prioritisation of critical systems, assets, and interdependencies; identification of threats, vulnerabilities, and countermeasures; layered cyber defences referencing current international standards such as zero-trust and defence-in-depth; continuous monitoring for timely incident detection; detailed incident handling procedures and a crisis response playbook; policies for secure information sharing with other financial institutions; a centralised automated technology asset inventory; and a dedicated cyber risk management function that analyses threats and escalates high-risk matters to senior management and the board.
evidence: CRF Master Document & Cyber Risk Context Assessment
Critical Systems, Information Asset Classification & Interdependency Register
Threat & Vulnerability Assessment Records with Countermeasure Mapping
Cyber Defence Architecture & Layered Controls Documentation
Security Operations & Continuous Monitoring Evidence

title: NCII Compliance Requirements
verbatim: A financial institution that is designated as a National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII) entity pursuant to the Cyber Security Act 2024 must ensure compliance to the requirements and guidelines applicable to NCII, including any additional directives and standards issued by the National Cyber Security Agency (NACSA).
translation: A financial institution designated as a National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII) entity under the Cyber Security Act 2024 must comply with all requirements, guidelines, directives, and standards applicable to NCII entities, including those issued by NACSA.
evidence: NCII Designation Certificate and Sector Classification Record
NACSA Directives and Standards Compliance Register
NCII Compliance Assessment and Gap Closure Evidence Pack

title: Cybersecurity Control Measures
verbatim: A financial institution must adopt robust control measures as outlined in Appendix 5 to enhance its resilience to cyber-attacks.

title: Crowdsourced Security Testing
verbatim: A financial institution may implement crowdsourced security testing programs as a complement to existing security assessments, in order to thoroughly test the security of their IT environment. The financial institution must engage reputable and credible service providers to facilitate the program.
translation: A financial institution may use crowdsourced security testing programs to supplement existing security assessments and more thoroughly test its IT environment. Where it does so, it must engage reputable and credible service providers to facilitate the program.

title: Cybersecurity Operations Responsibilities
translation: A financial institution must establish clear responsibilities for cybersecurity operations that include implementing appropriate mitigating measures across all seven phases of the cyber-attack lifecycle: reconnaissance, weaponisation, delivery, exploitation, installation, command and control, and exfiltration.

title: Continuous Security Monitoring and SOC
verbatim:
A financial institution must ensure continuous and proactive monitoring and timely detection of anomalous activities in its technology infrastructure to prevent potential compromise of its security controls or weakening of its security posture. This shall include-
(a) establishing a Security Operations Center (SOC) supported by competent resources and equipped with the necessary tools and technologies for proactive monitoring of its technology security posture;
(b) ensuring the scope of monitoring must cover all critical systems including the supporting infrastructure; and
(c) conducting regular review of its security posture via the conduct of a vulnerability assessment and penetration testing in line with Appendix 5.
translation: A financial institution must continuously and proactively monitor its technology infrastructure and detect anomalous activities in a timely manner to prevent compromise of its security controls. This requires establishing a Security Operations Centre (SOC) with competent staff and appropriate tools, ensuring all critical systems and supporting infrastructure are within scope of monitoring, and conducting regular vulnerability assessments and penetration testing in line with Appendix 5.
evidence: SOC Charter and Operating Model
SOC Tool and Technology Inventory
Monitoring Scope Register and Coverage Map
Security Alert and Incident Detection Records
Vulnerability Assessment Reports and Remediation Records

title: Cyber Threat Intelligence
verbatim: A financial institution must establish a process to collect, analyse and evaluate cyber threat information in relation to its environment (“cyber threat intelligence”) to promptly detect cyber threats, including data breach incidents and spread of misleading information in relation to the financial institution over the Internet.
translation: A financial institution must establish a process to collect, analyse, and evaluate cyber threat intelligence relevant to its environment so it can promptly detect cyber threats, including data breach incidents and the spread of misleading information about the institution online.
evidence: CTI Programme Policy and Procedure
Threat Intelligence Feed Subscription and Source Inventory

title: Cyber Crisis Management
verbatim: A financial institution must establish comprehensive cyber crisis management policies and procedures that incorporate cyber-attack scenarios and responses in the organisation's overall crisis management plan, escalation processes, business continuity and disaster recovery planning. This includes developing a clear communication plan for engaging shareholders, regulatory authorities, customers and employees in the event of a cyber incident.
translation: A financial institution must establish comprehensive cyber crisis management policies and procedures that incorporate cyber-attack scenarios into its overall crisis management plan, escalation processes, business continuity planning, and disaster recovery planning. This includes a clear communication plan for engaging shareholders, regulators, customers, and employees in the event of a cyber incident.
evidence: Cyber Crisis Management Policy and Procedures

title: Cyber Incident Response Plan
verbatim:
A financial institution must establish and implement a comprehensive Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP). The CIRP must address the following:
(a) Preparedness
Establish a clear governance process, reporting structure and roles and responsibilities of the Cyber Emergency Response Team (CERT) as well as invocation and escalation procedures in the event of an incident;
(b) Detection and analysis
Ensure effective and expedient processes for identifying points of compromise, assessing the extent of damage and preserving sufficient evidence for forensics purposes;
(c) Containment and eradication
Identify and implement remedial actions to prevent or minimise damage to the financial institution, contain and remove the known threats and resume business activities;
(d) Recovery
Implement multiple strategies including contingency plans as part of incident recovery to swiftly resume business operations and significantly enhance redundancy and resilience; and
(e) Post-incident activity
Conduct post-incident review incorporating lessons learned and develop long-term risk mitigations.
translation: A financial institution must establish and implement a comprehensive Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) covering five phases: Preparedness, including governance, roles, and escalation procedures for the Cyber Emergency Response Team (CERT); Detection and Analysis, covering identification of compromise points, damage assessment, and evidence preservation; Containment and Eradication, covering remedial actions to limit damage and remove threats; Recovery, including contingency plans to swiftly resume operations; and Post-Incident Activity, including lessons-learned reviews and long-term risk mitigations.
evidence: Cyber Incident Response Plan (CIRP) Master Document
CERT Governance Charter and Escalation Matrix
Incident Detection, Triage, and Forensic Preservation Procedures
Containment and Eradication Runbooks with Incident Execution Records
Incident Recovery Plans and Business Resumption Evidence

title: Out-of-Band Communication Infrastructure
verbatim: A financial institution shall establish a secure and reliable out-of-band communication infrastructure for both internal and external stakeholders to ensure continued coordination and communication if the primary communication infrastructure is compromised or rendered unavailable during a crisis.
translation: A financial institution must establish a secure and reliable out-of-band communication infrastructure for both internal and external stakeholders so that coordination and communication can continue if the primary communication infrastructure is compromised or unavailable during a crisis.
evidence: Out-of-Band Communication Policy and Framework
Out-of-Band Infrastructure Architecture and Asset Register

title: Annual Cyber Drill Exercise
verbatim:
A financial institution must conduct an annual cyber drill exercise to test the effectiveness of its CIRP including the out-of-band communication methods, based on various current and emerging threat scenarios (e.g. social engineering), with the involvement of key stakeholders including members of the board, senior management and relevant third party service providers. The result of the annual cyber drill exercise must be reported to the board in a timely manner. The out-of-band communication methods must also be tested regularly as part of the institutions cyber drill exercises. The test scenarios must include scenarios designed to test-
(a) the effectiveness of escalation, internal and external communication and decision-making processes that correspond to different impact levels of a cyber incident; and
(b) the readiness and effectiveness of CERT and relevant third party service providers in supporting the recovery process.
translation: A financial institution must conduct an annual cyber drill exercise to test the effectiveness of its CIRP, including out-of-band communication methods, using current and emerging threat scenarios and involving key stakeholders including board members, senior management, and relevant third-party service providers. Results must be reported to the board in a timely manner, and test scenarios must cover the effectiveness of escalation, communication, and decision-making processes at different impact levels, as well as the readiness of CERT and third-party providers in supporting recovery.
evidence: Annual Cyber Drill Exercise Programme and Scenario Design
Cyber Drill Exercise Execution Records and Attendance Evidence
After-Action Report and Gap Remediation Tracker
Board Reporting Record for Cyber Drill Results
Out-of-Band Communication Method Test Records

title: Cyber Insurance and Loss Provision
verbatim:
A financial institution shall review its loss provision arrangements to ensure its adequacy to cover cyber incidents based on its scenario analysis of extreme adverse events. Where cyber insurance is adopted to mitigate impact of cyber incidents, the financial institution shall-
(a) ensure that the scope of the insurance policy adequately covers the information security events and types of liability that the financial institution is exposed to;
(b) understand the terms and conditions of the insurance policy in relation to warranties, attestations or any responsibilities of the financial institution, have them reflected in the crisis response policies and procedures as appropriate and ensure that any changes to IT services and control measures do not result in unintended exclusions of cover; and
(c) ensure that any obligations imposed by the insurance policy (such as in relation to appointment of experts and accepting their recommendations during a cyber incident) do not impair its ability to act in the best interest of the financial institution and its customers. The financial institution shall anticipate and adequately manage any conflict of interest that may arise from the objective of the insurer to minimise the cost of its liability under the insurance policy.
translation: A financial institution must review its loss provision arrangements to ensure they are adequate to cover cyber incidents based on scenario analysis of extreme adverse events. Where cyber insurance is used, the institution must ensure the policy scope adequately covers relevant information security events and liabilities, that policy terms and conditions are reflected in crisis response procedures and do not create unintended gaps in cover, and that any obligations imposed by the insurer do not impair the institution's ability to act in the best interests of the institution and its customers, with conflicts of interest actively managed.
evidence: Cyber Loss Provision Review and Scenario Analysis Report
Cyber Insurance Policy Coverage Mapping and Gap Assessment
Insurance Policy Terms Integration into Crisis Response Procedures
IT Change Management Insurance Impact Assessment Records
Conflict of Interest Management Framework for Insurer Obligations

subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
title: Cyber Incident Notification to BNM
verbatim: A financial institution is required to notify the Bank of cyber incidents in adherence to the Bank's policy documents on Operational Risk Reporting – Part C, Business Continuity Management – Part C, Merchant Acquiring Services – paragraphs 19.25 to 19.26 and any other relevant policy documents specified by the Bank.
translation: A financial institution must notify Bank Negara Malaysia of cyber incidents in accordance with the Bank's policy documents on Operational Risk Reporting (Part C), Business Continuity Management (Part C), Merchant Acquiring Services (paragraphs 19.25 to 19.26), and any other relevant policy documents specified by the Bank.

subsection: Cyber Reporting and Threat Information Sharing
title: Cyber Threat Intelligence Sharing
verbatim: Subject to the applicable data protection laws, a financial institution must share cyber threat intelligence information with the industry on a timely basis via the relevant sharing platforms developed by the Bank, the industry, or law enforcement agencies. In addition, the financial institution must allocate resources to participate in any industry-wide initiatives aimed at improving collective threat intelligence capabilities.
translation: Subject to applicable data protection laws, a financial institution must share cyber threat intelligence with the industry in a timely manner through relevant sharing platforms developed by the Bank, the industry, or law enforcement agencies, and must allocate resources to participate in industry-wide initiatives aimed at improving collective threat intelligence capabilities.
evidence: CTI Sharing Policy and Procedures

Outbound CTI Sharing Activity Log
